perturbation.original_text: Mellet hereby irrevocably assigns such Product to Sweetskinz and authorizes Sweetskinz to execute on Mellet’s behalf such documents as it may deem necessary to confirm and protect its interests therein.
perturbation.changed_text: Mellet assigns such Product to Sweetskinz and Sweetskinz may execute documents to confirm its interests.
perturbation.explanation: The original text provides assurance that Sweetskinz can execute documents on Mellet's behalf to protect its interests, implying a more formal process and guarantee of cooperation. Removing the 'irrevocably' and 'authorizes Sweetskinz to execute on Mellet’s behalf such documents as it may deem necessary' weakens Mellet's obligation, introducing ambiguity and potentially violating Pennsylvania's Uniform Trade Secrets Act (PUTSA) which requires clear assignment of rights.
perturbation.law_explanation: PUTSA requires clear documentation of ownership and protection of trade secrets. By removing the stronger language ensuring Sweetskinz’s ability to act on Mellet's behalf, the agreement becomes less clear in assigning and protecting trade secrets, creating a potential conflict with the need for demonstrable assignment of rights under PUTSA.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: 12 Pennsylvania Consolidated Statutes § 5302 (2024) - Definitions :: 2024 Pennsylvania Consolidated Statutes :: U.S. Codes and Statutes :: U.S. Law :: Justia Go to Previous Versions of this Section 2024 Penn. Cons. Stat. (here) 2023 Penn. Cons. Stat. 2022 Penn. Cons. Stat. View All Versions 2024 Pennsylvania Consolidated & Unconsolidated Statutes Title 12 - COMMERCE AND TRADE Chapter 53 - Trade Secrets Section 5302 - Definitions Universal Citation: 12 PA Cons Stat § 5302 (2024) Learn more This media-neutral citation is based on the American Association of Law Libraries Universal Citation Guide and is not necessarily the official citation. Previous Next § 5302. Definitions. The following words and phrases when used in this chapter shall have the meanings given to them in this section unless the context clearly indicates otherwise: "Improper means." Includes, but is not limited to, theft, bribery, misrepresentation, breach or inducement of a breach of a duty to maintain secrecy or espionage through electronic or other means. "Misappropriation." Includes: (1) acquisition of a trade secret of another by a person who knows or has reason to know that the trade secret was acquired by improper means; or (2) disclosure or use of a trade secret of another without express or implied consent by a person who: (i) used improper means to acquire knowledge of the trade secret; (ii) at the time of disclosure or use, knew or had reason to know that his knowledge of the trade secret was: (A) derived from or through a person who had utilized improper means to acquire it; (B) acquired under circumstances giving rise to a duty to maintain its secrecy or limit its use; or (C) derived from or through a person who owed a duty to the person seeking relief to maintain its secrecy or limit its use; or (iii) before a material change of his position, knew or had reason to know that it was a trade secret and that knowledge of it had been acquired by accident or mistake. "Person." A natural person, corporation, business trust, estate, trust
perturbation.justification: The changed text weakens the assignment of rights and the ability of Sweetskinz to protect its interests, potentially conflicting with the requirements of PUTSA for clear documentation of ownership and protection of trade secrets. While Scraped Law Snippet 1 is missing, Scraped Law Snippet 2 defines 'Misappropriation' and 'Improper Means,' implying the need for clear assignment and protection of trade secrets to prevent such actions. The original text ensured Sweetskinz could act on Mellet's behalf, while the modified text removes this assurance, creating ambiguity and potentially hindering the protection of trade secrets as required by PUTSA. This omission directly affects the enforceability of the trade secret assignment.

perturbation.original_text: Each Party agrees at all times during the Term to hold in the strictest confidence, and not use, except for the benefit of the Parties or to disclose, transfer or reveal, directly or indirectly to any person or entity any confidential information.
perturbation.changed_text: Each Party agrees during the Term to hold in confidence, and not use or disclose to any entity any confidential information.
perturbation.explanation: The omission of 'at all times,' 'strictest,' 'except for the benefit of the Parties', 'transfer or reveal, directly or indirectly,' weakens the confidentiality clause. This creates ambiguity and reduces the enforceability of the non-disclosure agreement, creating legal risk under Pennsylvania law regarding the protection of trade secrets.
perturbation.law_explanation: Pennsylvania law requires reasonable efforts to maintain the secrecy of trade secrets. By weakening the confidentiality clause, the agreement now indicates less effort will be made to maintain secrecy of information, potentially conflicting with the 'reasonable efforts' standard and making it harder to demonstrate misappropriation under PUTSA.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: 12 Pennsylvania Consolidated Statutes § 5304 (2024) - Damages :: 2024 Pennsylvania Consolidated Statutes :: U.S. Codes and Statutes :: U.S. Law :: Justia Go to Previous Versions of this Section 2024 Penn. Cons. Stat. (here) 2023 Penn. Cons. Stat. 2022 Penn. Cons. Stat. View All Versions 2024 Pennsylvania Consolidated & Unconsolidated Statutes Title 12 - COMMERCE AND TRADE Chapter 53 - Trade Secrets Section 5304 - Damages Universal Citation: 12 PA Cons Stat § 5304 (2024) Learn more This media-neutral citation is based on the American Association of Law Libraries Universal Citation Guide and is not necessarily the official citation. Previous Next § 5304. Damages. (a) Monetary damages.-- Except to the extent that a material and prejudicial change of position prior to acquiring knowledge or reason to know of misappropriation renders a monetary recovery inequitable, a complainant is entitled to recover damages for misappropriation. Damages can include both the actual loss caused by misappropriation and the unjust enrichment caused by misappropriation that is not taken into account in computing actual loss. In lieu of damages measured by any other methods, the damages caused by misappropriation may be measured by imposition of liability for a reasonable royalty for a misappropriator's unauthorized disclosure or use of a trade secret. (b) Exemplary damages.-- If willful and malicious misappropriation exists, the court may award exemplary damages in an amount not exceeding twice any award made under subsection (a). perturbation.justification: The modification weakens the confidentiality clause, which could contradict Pennsylvania law requiring 'reasonable efforts' to maintain trade secret secrecy. By omitting 'at all times', 'strictest', 'except for the benefit of the Parties', and 'transfer or reveal, directly or indirectly,' the changed text indicates less effort to maintain secrecy. This could make it harder to demonstrate misappropriation under PUTSA, as highlighted in the Law Explanation. Scraped Law Snippet 2, 12 Pennsylvania Consolidated Statutes § 5304 (2024), discusses damages for misappropriation, implying a legal expectation of preventing misappropriation through reasonable measures. Weakening the confidentiality clause potentially undermines these reasonable measures.

perturbation.original_text: If Sweetskinz so elects, Sweetskinz shall be entitled, in addition to all other remedies available, including but not limited to actual, compensatory and punitive damages, or to specifically enforce the performance by Mellet and to enjoin the violation by Mellet of any provision hereof.
perturbation.changed_text: If Sweetskinz elects, Sweetskinz shall be entitled to remedies available or to enforce the performance by Mellet of any provision hereof.
perturbation.explanation: The original text explicitly includes 'actual, compensatory and punitive damages' as remedies, enhancing Sweetskinz's protection. Removing this list and the phrase 'in addition to all other' limits the explicitly stated remedies available to Sweetskinz and weakens the deterrent effect, potentially conflicting with Pennsylvania law regarding remedies for breach of contract and trade secret misappropriation.
perturbation.law_explanation: Pennsylvania follows the Restatement (Second) of Contracts, which acknowledges various remedies for breach, including damages. By omitting the specific mention of damages, the contract appears to restrict the remedies available to Sweetskinz compared to what's generally allowed under Pennsylvania law, creating a possible point of legal challenge.

perturbation.justification: The original contract explicitly lists 'actual, compensatory and punitive damages' as available remedies for Sweetskinz. The changed text omits this specific list, potentially restricting the remedies available to Sweetskinz compared to what's generally allowed. Although the scraped snippets do not directly address the remedies available, the Law Explanation highlights that Pennsylvania follows the Restatement (Second) of Contracts, which acknowledges various remedies for breach, including damages. By omitting the specific mention of damages, the contract appears to restrict remedies available to Sweetskinz compared to what's generally allowed under Pennsylvania law, creating a point of legal challenge, thus a contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: This Agreement is governed by the laws of Pennsylvania. If any of this Agreement is more restrictive than permitted by law in any jurisdiction in which enforcement is sought, this Agreement will be limited only to the extent necessary to bring this Agreement within the law of such jurisdiction and other provisions of the Agreement will remain in force.
perturbation.changed_text: This Agreement is governed by the laws of Pennsylvania.
perturbation.explanation: The original text contains a severability clause, ensuring that if any part of the agreement is deemed too restrictive, it will be limited to comply with the law, while the rest of the agreement remains in force. Removing this clause creates a risk that an overly broad or restrictive provision could invalidate the entire agreement under Pennsylvania law, leaving Sweetskinz without any protection.
perturbation.law_citation: Robert H. Stremmel Acquisitions, Inc. v. Penn Nat. Gaming, Inc., 937 A.2d 494 (Pa. Super. 2007)
perturbation.law_explanation: Pennsylvania courts generally favor severability clauses to uphold as much of a contract as possible. By removing the severability clause, the contract now risks being entirely invalidated if a single provision is found to be overly restrictive, conflicting with the principle that courts should strive to enforce contracts to the extent permitted by law. The absence of a severability clause makes the agreement riskier under Pennsylvania contract law.
perturbation.justification: The omission of the severability clause creates a legal contradiction. The original text ensured that if any part of the agreement was overly restrictive, it would be limited to comply with the law, with the rest of the agreement remaining in force. By removing this clause, the entire agreement risks being invalidated if a single provision is found overly restrictive. The Law Explanation supports this, stating that Pennsylvania courts favor severability clauses to uphold as much of a contract as possible. The absence of the clause conflicts with the principle that courts should strive to enforce contracts to the extent permitted by law.
